coredata.dc:title: A dual-response ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on metal-organic frameworks for ultrasensitive visual detection of 4-nitrophenol in environments
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Jinli
Zhou, Shu
Zhao, Pengfei
Wu, Xiaodan
Tang, Sisi
Chen, Shan
Yang, Zhaoxia
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: A dual-response ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on visible/near-infrared emission was established for ultrasensitive, selective and visual detection 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). The molecularly imprinted polymer was incorporated in the ratiometric sensing system consisting of visible emission carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) and near-infrared emission cadmium telluride (CdTe) quantum dots. The CDs@ZIF-8 enhanced the emission of CDs and the fluorescence sensing performance. Compared to short wavelength of fluorophore, the near-infrared emission CdTe is less interference caused by auto-fluorescence of sample. The ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor exhibited dual response for 4-NP at 420 nm and 703 nm and a wide concentration response range. Moreover, a good linear response was existed in the two concentration ranges of 0.1 pM–3.0 pM and 0.05 μM–30 μM with the detection limit of 0.08 pM and 0.05 μΜ, respectively. Significantly, the fluorescence color changes can be observed from purple to pink to red with the naked eye. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the ratimetric fluorescence imprinted sensor was discussed in detail. The ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor was used to detect 4-NP in various real samples with satisfactory recoveries of 97.5–106.3%, which provided an interesting avenue for the rapid detection of pollutant with high sensitivity, high selectivity and visualization in real environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Metal-organic framework
Molecularly imprinted polymers
4-Nitrophenol

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence spectrum analysis and morphology analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra and fluorescence color of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP with different ratios of CDs@ZIF-8 and CdTe QDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of ZIF-8, CDs and CDs@ZIF-8. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8 (a), CdTe QDs (b) and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP (c) excited at 375 nm and the inset shows the digital images of CDs@ZIF-8 (a), CdTe QDs (b) and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP (c) under UV irradiation at 365 nm. TEM images of (D) CdTe QDs (inset: partial enlargement), (E) CDs@ZIF-8 and (F) CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP (inset: the corresponding close-up TEM image of a single particle). SEM images of (G) CDs@ZIF-8 and (H) CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence response efficiency and intensity change (inset) at different time of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP after adding 4-NP (1.5 pM). (B) The fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP at different pHs (4.0–12.0). (C) The fluorescence color of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP at different pHs (4.0–12.0) under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (D) The fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP by 4-NP (1.5 pM) at different pHs (5.0–11.0). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) corresponding quenching efficiency (Log[(F420/F703)0/(F420/F703)]) of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP with addition of 4-NP concentration ranged from 0.1 pM to 30 μM (inset of A: the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP in both VIS and NIR emission in the range of 0.05 μM–30 μM). Fluorescence emission spectra of (C) CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and (D) CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP responded to the 4-NP (0.1 pM–3.0 pM). (E) The slope of fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP toward 4-NP (0.1 pM–16.0 pM). (F) The fluorescence spectra and fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) of CDs/CdTe@MIP toward 4-NP (0.05 μM–30 μM). The photography images in the inner (C, D) are the fluorescence color change (left to right) with the addition of 4-NP in the concentration ranges of 0.1 pM–3.0 pM under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence emission (a) and excitation (b) spectra of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP, UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NP (c) and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP before (d) and after (e) 4-NP added. (B) Schematic of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 4-NP.

(A) The fluorescence responses at visible (420 nm) and near infrared emission (703 nm) of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP toward 15 μM of six phenols and 40 mg L−1 of interfering ions. (B) Fluorescence responses and fluorescence color of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP toward 1.5 pM of 4-NP and other five phenols. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration for fabrication process of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP.

head.ce:title: A dual-response ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on metal-organic frameworks for ultrasensitive visual detection of 4-nitrophenol in environments

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan, 416000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jishou University, Jishou, 416000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan, 416000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-response ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on visible/near-infrared emission was established for ultrasensitive, selective and visual detection 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). The molecularly imprinted polymer was incorporated in the ratiometric sensing system consisting of visible emission carbon dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) and near-infrared emission cadmium telluride (CdTe) quantum dots. The CDs@ZIF-8 enhanced the emission of CDs and the fluorescence sensing performance. Compared to short wavelength of fluorophore, the near-infrared emission CdTe is less interference caused by auto-fluorescence of sample. The ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor exhibited dual response for 4-NP at 420 nm and 703 nm and a wide concentration response range. Moreover, a good linear response was existed in the two concentration ranges of 0.1 pM–3.0 pM and 0.05 μM–30 μM with the detection limit of 0.08 pM and 0.05 μΜ, respectively. Significantly, the fluorescence color changes can be observed from purple to pink to red with the naked eye. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the ratimetric fluorescence imprinted sensor was discussed in detail. The ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor was used to detect 4-NP in various real samples with satisfactory recoveries of 97.5–106.3%, which provided an interesting avenue for the rapid detection of pollutant with high sensitivity, high selectivity and visualization in real environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrated phenols with adverse effects on air quality, climate change and human health have attracted attention in recent years due to the anthropogenic activities (Li et al., 2020b; Cheng et al., 2021). 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP), one of the typical representatives, has high toxicity and carcinogenicity even at lower concentration (Yu et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020b). Currently, determination methods for 4-NP included gas chromatography-mass, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, etc (Harrison et al., 2005; Li et al., 2020b). However, these detection methods have some limitations involving expensive instrument, skilled personnel, time consuming and poor selectivity.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), mimicking the high selectivity of the antibody, have been employed as recognition media owing to their tailor-made binding sites (Yin et al., 2015; Lv et al., 2018). Fluorescence-based MIPs (FL-MIPs) are superior for the analysis of trace pollutants because of the high sensitivity, excellent specificity, and simple detection process (Chen et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2018). However, most of the previous reported MIP-based fluorescence sensors were based on single wavelength emission (Liu et al., 2010; Cai et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2017), which exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity but the detection accuracy can be affected easily by environmental conditions and interferences. Interestingly, a ratiometric FL-MIPs sensor with two wavelength emissions could eliminate these interferences through self-calibration of two different bands (Amjadi and Jalili, 2017; Liu et al., 2018). Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOF) has attracted much attention due to their high accuracy, high sensitivity and visual analysis (Wang et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2021). Currently, the typical LMOF is encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). ZIF-8 with high porosity and large specific surface area not only solved the self-quenching of CDs caused by aggregation, but also concentrated the analyte to improve the sensitivity of detection (Chen et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2021). However, most of ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on LMOF are visible (VIS) emission, which are susceptible to interference such as background fluorescence and the environmentally-induced (Ding et al., 2021). Near infrared (NIR, 650–1400 nm) emission cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs) have great potential for preparation ratiometric fluorescence sensor due to the low degree of auto-fluorescence and strong anti-interference (Chen and Liang, 2014; Cong et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2021). However, the LMOF-based VIS/NIR dual-response ratiometric FL-MIPs sensor for visual detection is few.

Herein, a VIS/NIR dual-response ratiometric FL-MIPs sensor based on LMOF was proposed for rapid visual determination of 4-NP with the advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity. Firstly, CDs were incorporated into ZIF-8 by in situ incorporation to obtain the VIS emission CDs@ZIF-8. Then NIR emitting CdTe QDs were entrapped in the CDs@ZIF-8. Finally, an imprinted layer with specific recognition toward 4-NP was prepared on the surface of LMOF through one-step sol-gel polymerization. The CDs@ZIF-8 with high fluorescence intensity at visible emission improved the sensitivity, NIR emission CdTe QDs enhanced the anti-interference ability of environment and MIPs ensured the high selective recognition of 4-NP. Interestingly, the dual-response ratiometric FL-MIPs sensor can achieve the visualization of fluorescence color changes through different degrees of response of two fluorescence signals, providing more richer and accurate information for the detection.
The experimental section was shown in the supporting information.

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor which combined CDs@ZIF-8, CdTe QDs and MIP was synthesized for the rapid detection of 4-NP. As shown in Scheme 1 , the visible emission CDs@ZIF-8 was prepared via in situ incorporation of CDs in ZIF-8. Then the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted polymers on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8 coated with CdTe QDs were prepared through sol-gel method in which the cocondensation reaction between APTES and TEOS was catalyzed by aqueous ammonia solution in the presence of 4-NP. The strong noncovalent interaction between amino group of APTES and hydroxyl portion of 4-NP supported the specific recognition of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP toward 4-NP. After 4-NP was removed by methanol/acetic acid mixture (9:1, v/v), the empty imprinted cavities were obtained in the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP.

In order to achieve the optimal fluorescence response and visual detection for 4-NP, the ratio of CDs@ZIF-8 and CdTe QDs was optimized. The fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP prepared with different ratios of CDs@ZIF-8 and CdTe QDs were shown in Fig. 1 A. The results showed that when 10 mg of CDs@ZIF-8 and 3 mL of CdTe QDs (curve b) were selected, the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor showed purple fluorescence under UV lamp at 365 nm (insert b). When 4-NP was added, the fluorescence color of the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor changed from purple to red under UV lamp (Inset in Scheme 1), achieving the visual detection of 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectrum analysis and morphology analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 composite were shown in Fig. 1B. Visible emission CDs showed a maximum fluorescence emission at 475 nm under excited at 375 nm. However, the pure ZIF-8 has no obvious emission under excited at 375 nm. Interestingly, the CDs@ZIF-8 showed a fluorescence enhancement and blue-shift (from 475 nm to 450 nm) under excited at 375 nm. This phenomenon was ascribed to the coordination-induced emission (CIE), where CDs and dimethylimidazole ligands coordinated with zinc ions during the formation of CDs@ZIF-8 (Yin et al., 2019). When CDs were in situ incorporated, the emission from CDs was enhanced due to the coordination of CDs to zinc ions. In addition, CDs with rigid network structure formed by a compact network of short polyamide chains were twisted to adapt to the CDs@ZIF-8 rigid structure (Vallan et al., 2018), resulting in the blue-shift of CIE, which has been reported in the previous reports (Yin et al., 2019).

The fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-8, CdTe QDs and the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP were recorded in Fig. 1C. The CDs@ZIF-8 composite exhibited fluorescence spectrum with emission peak at 450 nm upon excitation at 375 nm (curve a), which showed intense bright blue luminescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm (insert a). Meanwhile, the red fluorescence CdTe QDs (insert b) showed fluorescence maximum at 675 nm (curve b). After the two fluorescence materials were incorporated in the MIP, the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor showed visible/near-infrared emission peaks at 420 nm and 703 nm (curve c) under excited at 375 nm, in which the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP displayed purple fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm (insert c). The phenomenon of VIS emission blue-shift (from 450 nm to 420 nm) and NIR emission red-shift (from 675 nm to 703 nm) may be attributed to the CIE and attachment of imprinted polymer onto the CDs@ZIF-8 surface (Morris-Cohen et al., 2012; Cong et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2014; Yin et al., 2019). These results indicated that the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP was successfully prepared for ratiometric fluorescence determination of 4-NP.

The morphologies of CdTe QDs, CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP were characterized by SEM and TEM in detail. As shown in Fig. 1D, the CdTe QDs dispersed well with the uniform size distributions ranged from 4 to 6 nm (inset of Fig. 1D). As shown in Fig. 1E, the CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a nearly spherical hexagonal prism with an average size of 40 nm, which was in keeping with the SEM image of CDs@ZIF-8 shown in Fig. 1G. Accompanied by the growth of MIP on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8, the spherical imprinted polymer was observed in Fig. 1F and the sizes were expanded to 100–130 nm (inset of Fig. 1F), which was identical of the results of SEM image of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP shown in Fig. 1H. In addition, the interior of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP still keep the primary framework of CDs@ZIF-8. The above data confirmed the successful preparation of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP.

The crystal structures of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and the results were shown in Fig. S1. Both ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 showed mostly similar high crystallinity and sharp diffraction peaks, in which typical characteristic diffraction peaks (from 10 to 20) were consistent with the previous literature (Qin et al., 2020). It verified that doping of CDs did not destroy the general structure of ZIF-8, which was owing to ultra-small size CDs not enough to break the rigid structure of ZIF-8 (Vallan et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2019). Furthermore, the intensity of diffraction peak of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP was significantly reduced compared with the CDs@ZIF-8. Moreover, one broad characteristic diffraction peak (2θ=23°) of amorphous SiO2 was observed, which similar to those of literature data (Li et al., 2016; Cao et al., 2021). Based on the above analysis, the imprinted silica layer may disrupt the order in the MOFs, but the relatively short response time (2 min) compared to previous report (Lu and Xu, 2017) indicated the presence of open structures that allow rapid diffusion. Thus, the above results indicated the successful synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP, which was accorded with the results of the morphology characterization of SEM and TEM.

The functional groups of CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. S2, peaks presented at 3144 cm−1 and 2918 cm−1 in the spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H in aromatic and aliphatic group, respectively. Stretching vibration of C=O at 1640 cm−1 was mainly contributed from CDs and peaks centered at 1415 cm−1 and 990 cm−1 belonged to the tensile vibration and plane bending vibration of imidazole rings. In addition, an absorption peak at 422 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of Zn-N. The results indicated the formation of CDs@ZIF-8. In addition, the similarity of the major bands was existed between CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP. The characteristic peaks around 460 cm−1 and 786 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching vibration of Si-O and the wide and strong absorption peak at 1046 cm−1 was characteristic signal of typical asymmetric Si-O-Si stretching, indicating the presence of SiO2 matrices. Moreover, The stretching vibration bands of N-H around 3422 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 and aliphatic C-H stretching band at 2924 cm−1 suggested the presence of the aminopropyl group of APTES, which further identified the sol-gel polymerization reaction have successfully completed. The data confirmed the construction of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP was successfully.

In order to sensitively detect 4-NP, determination conditions which may affect the fluorescence response efficiency, such as the excitation wavelength, response time and pH value were optimized. Under excited at 375 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of visible light was at 420 nm and the maximum emission peak in near-infrared region was at 703 nm (Fig. S3). Thus, the excitation wavelength at 375 nm was used in the further experiments. The data for fluorescence response vs. time after the addition of 4-NP (1.5 pM) was shown in Fig. 2 A. The fluorescence intensity and response efficiency of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP was changed continuously and then rapidly achieved stability within 2.0 min. The shorter response time of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor toward 4-NP was attributed to the large surface area of CDs@ZIF-8, which could provide more effective imprinted recognition sites for fluorescence response toward 4-NP (Liu et al., 2019).

The fluorescence intensities of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP at different pH values in the range of 4.0–12.0 were studied. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the visible (420 nm) and near-infrared (703 nm) emission reached the maximum value when pH was 7.0. Meanwhile, Fig. 2C showed that the purple fluorescence emission of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP was the highest at pH 7.0 under UV irradiation at 365 nm. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2D, the response efficiency of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP toward 1.5 pM of 4-NP at different pH values (5.0–11.0) was examined and the results showed that the quenching efficiency was the maximum at pH 7.0. In addition, the solution of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP became transparent at pH 4.0–5.0 (Fig. S4), indicating the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP structure may be damaged, which was consistent with the previous literature (Bai et al., 2020). Thus, aqueous solution at pH 7.0 was used for the subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP for detection of 4-NP was shown in Fig. 3 A. The detection of 4-NP was based on accumulative procedure to analyze the gradual recognition process between imprinted sites and target (Xu et al., 2016). In the experiment, the sensor was exposed to subsequent increased concentrations without removing the previously recognized 4-NP. The CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP showed dual-response toward 4-NP (from 0.1 pM to 30 μM) in the VIS and NIR emission. Remarkably, the VIS emission peak (420 nm) gradually red shifted (from 420 to 475 nm) while the NIR emission peak (703 nm) was unchanged. The CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP also showed the similar results (Fig. S5). The corresponding quenching efficiency (Log [(F420/F703)0/(F420/F703)], where (F420/F703)0 and (F420/F703) are the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 420 nm–703 nm in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively) of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP toward 4-NP in the wide range of 0.1 pM–3.0 μM was shown in Fig. 3B. A good linear response was existed in the two concentration ranges of 0.1 pM–3.0 pM and 0.05 μM–30 μM. However, the fluorescence response in the range of 3.0 pM-0.05 μM was nonlinear (shown in Fig. S6). When 4-NP concentrations were pM ranges (0.1 pM–3.0 pM), only the fluorescence CDs located in the imprinted layer on surface of CDs@ZIF-8 could be sensitively quenched. In the lower concentration range (3.0 pM-0.05 μM) of 4-NP, the fluorescence change was small (black circles in Fig. 3A) with a nonlinear response, which may be due to the fact that the low concentration of 4-NP could not fully contact the internal fluorescence signal in CDs@ZIF-8, which was consistent with previous report (Yang et al., 2019; Bogireddy et al., 2019). When 4-NP concentration was μM ranges (0.05 μM–30 μM), the internal fluorescence signal in CDs@ZIF-8 was responded rapidly and showing obvious red-shifted (420 nm–475 nm) at VIS emission, which may be that the coordination between CD and zinc ion was affected with the addition of 4-NP (Vallan et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2019). Moreover, the sensitivity and visual ability of the VIS/NIR ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor for quantitative analysis of 4-NP was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3C, when 4-NP (0.1 pM–3.0 pM) was mixed with the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP, the fluorescence intensities at VIS emission (420 nm) gradually decreased while the intensities at NIR emission (703 nm) were only slightly altered with the increment of 4-NP concentrations. In addition, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3C, the fluorescence color changed from purple to pink to red with the addition of 4-NP in the concentration ranges of 0.1 pM–3.0 pM under UV irradiation at 365 nm. In addition, the fluorescence intensity and color change of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP was not obvious (inset of Fig. 3D), which was attributed to no specific recognition site. Thus the sensitive and visual detection of 4-NP can be achieved.

The slope of fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP toward 4-NP (0.1 pM–16.0 pM) was shown in Fig. 3E. The logarithm of fluorescence intensity ratio (Log [(F420/F703)0/(F420/F703)]) of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP exhibited excellent linear relationship (y= 0.0318 C + 0.0031, R2 = 0.995) in 4-NP concentration range of 0.1 pM–3.0 pM with the detection limit of 0.08 pM based on triple signal-to-noise ratio (3S/N), and the sensing performance was higher than that of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP, which was attributed to the imprinted cavities on the surface of MOF-based sensor provided higher accessibility to achieve the higher sensitive detection in lower concentration (Yang et al., 2019). In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F=1+KSVC, where F0 and F are the fluorescent intensities of sensor in the absence and presence of 4-NP. C is the concentration of 4-NP. KSV is the fluorescence quenching constant) was used to calculate the imprinting factor (IF, IF= Ksv,MIP/Ksv,NIP). According with linear fitting curve showed in Fig. S7, the imprinting factor (IF) was calculated as 3.27, which indicated the good imprinting effect of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP toward 4-NP (Zhang et al., 2021).

In addition, the fluorescence response of CDs/CdTe@MIP toward 4-NP in the range of 0.05 μΜ-30.0 μΜ was shown in Fig. 3F. The results showed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP in both VIS and NIR emission (inset of Fig. 3A) was higher than that of the CDs/CdTe@MIP (inset of Fig. 3F), and the LOD of 0.05 μΜ decreased about 11 times due to the presence of ZIF-8. Compared with the CDs/CdTe@MIP, the high sensitivity of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP toward 4-NP was ascribed to the combined effect of the high selective imprinting recognition effect of imprinted materials and the strong signal-amplification effect of ZIF-8 (Xu et al., 2016).

The dual-responses fluorescence quenching mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor toward 4-NP was discussed in detail. Generally, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was attributed to either photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between fluorescence signal and analytes (Li et al., 2016; Ensafi et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 4 A, the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor showed dual emission peaks at 420 nm and 703 nm (curve a) under excited at 375 nm (curve b). However, the UV absorption peaks of 4-NP were 220 nm and 320 nm (curve c). The results excluded the mechanism of FRET because of the absence of overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP (curve a) and the UV absorption spectra of 4-NP (curve c) (Liu et al., 2010; Li et al., 2016, 2020a). Based on the above analysis and previous reports, the fluorescence quenching can be inferred by the electron transfer between the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and 4-NP, in which amino groups (NH2) in binding site of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP interact with the functional groups of 4-NP (such as hydroxyl and nitro group). The strong interaction was proved by the two significantly different spectra of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP before (curve d) and after (curve e) 4-NP added.

Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4B, molecular orbital theory (Liu et al., 2010) was used to elucidate the fluorescence quenching process of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP by 4-NP. The electrons of ratio fluorescence signals were excited from ground-state (valence band) to the excited state (conduction band) by accepting the excited energy. On the one hand, in the absence of 4-NP (Fig. 4B, a), the excited electrons of CDs@ZIF-8 (VIS) and CdTe QDs (NIR) got back to the valence band following the solid arrow, together with blue and red fluorescence. Moreover, the obtained ratio fluorescence signals of the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor showed purple fluorescence because of the combination between CDs@ZIF-8 and CdTe QDs. On the other hand, in the presence of 4-NP (Fig. 4B, b), the strong interaction of hydrogen bonds leaded to the electron transfer between ratio fluorescence signals and 4-NP (Liu et al., 2010). Since the UV absorption peaks of 4-NP are 220 nm and 320 nm, which was close to the band gap of CDs in the CDs@ZIF-8, the excited electron at conduction band of CDs in the CDs@ZIF-8 can directly transfer to the LUMO of 4-NP. Moreover, the energy bands of 4-NP were higher than that of CdTe QDs, which is consistent with the literature reports (Liu et al., 2010; Li et al., 2020a), leading to the excited electrons at conduction band of CdTe QDs be prone to transition into the LUMO of the 4-NP. Based on the above analysis, the PET causes the fluorescence quenching of ratio fluorescence signals of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP.

To evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor, the fluorescence responses of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP and CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP toward 4-NP and five analogs as well as interfering ions (40 mg L−1) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence quenching response of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP by 4-NP (15 μM) was the strongest and the fluorescence response of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP was greater than that of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP in both visible and near-infrared region. Hence, the results indicated that the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP showed good selectivity toward 4-NP. Moreover, the selectivity and fluorescence color variation of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP for 4-NP and five analogs in the linear range of 0.1 pM–3.0 pM were discussed in Fig. 5B. The logarithm of fluorescence intensity ratio (Log ((F420/F703)0/(F420/F703)) of the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor were recorded in the presence of 1.5 pM of six analysts. The results showed that the fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP toward 4-NP was higher than that of other analogs, while the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@NIP showed no significant difference in fluorescence response toward all analytes. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color under UV irradiation at 365 nm was changed from purple to pink after adding 1.5 pM of 4-NP.

To evaluate the application of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP, determination of 4-NP in PM2.5, tap water and real lake water sample was performed. Spiking recovery studies were carried out by adding different concentrations (μM) of 4-NP into the samples and the results were compared with HPLC-UV. As shown in Table S1, the recoveries ranged from 97.5% to 106.3%, which was corresponding to the results of HPLC method. The data indicated the usefulness of the fluorescence imprinted sensor for the trace determination of 4-NP in complex real samples.

The performance comparison of different methods for the determination of 4-NP in the various real samples was listed in Table S2. The CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP in this paper showed the lower LOD values (0.08 pM and 0.05 μΜ) and wider linear range (0.1 pM–3.0 pM and 0.05 μM–30 μM), which was superior to other fluorescence and electrochemistry sensor. To sum up, the developed ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor provided a simple and fast determination method for selective and sensitive visual detection of 4-NP in complex environment.

Since the fluorescence stability of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP is an important property in the fluorescence detection, the fluorescence intensity ratios (F420/F703) of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP ratiometric sensor every 15 min interval were investigated. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 5 measurements within 60 min was 3.6%, while the purple fluorescence color was no change under irradiated 365 nm UV lamp. Besides, when the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP was kept at 4 °C for four weeks, the fluorescence intensity decreased less than 5.0% of original intensity.

In addition, the reproducibility of the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP ratiometric sensor was also investigated by removal-rebinding cycle test with the same CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP. The RSD for five repeated analysis was 4.5%. These above data indicated the CDs@ZIF-8/CdTe@MIP ratiometric sensor displayed high stability and excellent reproducibility.

In summary, a dual-response ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on VIS emission CD@ZIF-8 (420 nm) and NIR emission CdTe QDs (703 nm) was prepared for the rapid detection of trace 4-NP. The CD@ZIF-8 enhanced the emission of CDs and the fluorescence sensing performance. The fluorescence imprinted sensor exhibited dual-response at 420 nm and 703 nm and a wide concentration response range. Moreover, a good linear response was existed in the two concentration ranges of 0.1–3.0 pM and 0.05–30 μM with the detection limit of 0.08 pM and 0.05 μΜ, respectively. The variation of ratio fluorescence intensity leads to an obvious fluorescence color change from purple to pink to red, providing the visual detection of 4-NP. The ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor offered the feasibility of rapid, visual, selective and sensitive determination of 4-NP in real environment with satisfactory recoveries of 97.5–106.3%.

Jinli Fu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shu Zhou: Resources, Methodology. Pengfei Zhao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaodan Wu: Supervision. Sisi Tang: Validation. Shan Chen: Supervision, Project administration. Zhaohui Zhang: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan province (No.2020JJ4500).
